image.url: img/map[comment_count:%!s(bool=true) gravatar:%!s(bool=false) main_menu:map[align:l show_logo:%!s(bool=true)] reading_time:%!s(bool=true) section_pager:%!s(bool=false) shape:circle sharing:%!s(bool=false)]
image.title: Projects

item.title: Thesis report Week#7
item.description:
Tracking a single target in clutter

Problem formulation:

Perform state estimation of a single target using observations of uncertain origin.

  • Target's tracked state at sample time $k$ is, $\mathbf{x}_k=(x,y,V,h,\omega)^T$ with respect to the self-vehicle sea-keeping frame.

  • Assume its track is initialized tracks i.e the problem is that of track maintenance .

  • Observations $z_k = h(\mathbf{x}_k) + w_k$ from various sensors, defined my a corresponding measurement model and the target's state.

  • $w$ is gaussian white and its covariance matrix depends on the sensor properties.

  • Detection probability $P_D$ less than unity, i.e. there exists the possibility of the sensor not returning an observation that belongs to the target, due to various reasons.

  • Non zero $P_{FD}$, ie the sensor can provide false positive observations that do not originate from the tracked target, usually due to clutter.

  • Possibility for multiple validated observations for a single target.

Clutter model

The target tracker estimates the target state such as position and orientation from the noisy measurement observations originating from various object's sources such as real targets, clouds, terrain reflections and thermal noise. The unwanted measurements are referred as clutter measurements.[source]

Most of the sensors are finite in their perception resolution, that is they have $N$ resolution cells. A detection is declared in a cell if the output of the signal for the specific cell exceeds a threshold. If the sensor points to a region which is not occupied by a target, then false detections can still occur due to sensor noise or background noise. Usually filtering is performed with observations instead of detections, that is with the output of the raw-data preprocessor.

In radar tracking for instance, the preprocessor might run clustering algorithms on the raw detections and provide the mean of each cluster as a different observation. The preprocessing of the raw data should remove what was defined as clutter, nevertheless preprocessors still produce false positive observations. Observations(post-processed information from raw data) can have a similar clutter model.

Assumptions

  • The probability of a false positive observation inside the sensors grid cell is $P_{FA} = p$ in each cell
  • The events of observations in each cell are independent of each other.

Then the probability mass function (pmf) of the number of false alarms in these $N$ cells, follows a binomial(Bernouli) distribution

\[ P\{n_{FA}=m\} = \mu_{FA}(m) = \begin{pmatrix}N \\m \end{pmatrix} p^m (1-p)^{N-m} \]

If $V$ is the volume of the $N$ cells under consideration, then $\lambda$ is the spatial density of the false alarms

\[ \lambda = \frac{E{n_{FA}}}{V} = \frac{Np}{V} \]

If $Np >1$ then the Poisson distribution can be approximated by

\[ \mu_{FA}(m) \approx e^{-Np}\frac{(Np)^m}{m!} \]

thus the number of false alarms(clutter observations) in a volume $V$ given a spatial density of clutter observations $\lambda$ is

\[ \mu_{FA}(m) \approx e^{-\lambda V}\frac{(\lambda V)^m}{m!} \]

The justification of using the above clutter model, is that the pdf of a false observation is uniform

\[ p(z|{z = \text{false measurement}}) = \frac{1}{V} \]

where $V$ corresponds to the subspace volume in which the observation is originated from.

Approaches to filtering

  • Non-Bayesian techniques
    • Nearest Neighbour association
    • Strongest Neighbour association (assuming intensity information)
    • Track-Split approach
  • Bayesian techniques
    • Probabilistic Data Association filter
    • Optimal Bayesian approach, multiple hypothesis (MH)

Probabilistic Data Association Filter (PDAF) overview

This filter is based on the KF/EKF algorithm, it keeps the update equations the same but is probabilistically fusing observations within the target's validating region into a single fused innovation update step.

Modeling assumptions of the PDAF

  • Single target of interested modeled by white-noise driven state motion modele as in KF/EFK
  • The target's track is already initialized
  • At each sensor sample time, a validation region(gate) to filter the highly unprobable observations is created.
  • The target exhibits a detection probability at eash sensor scan $P_D$
  • The remaining observations(not originating from the target) are assumed due to false detection or clutter, and are assumed to be of uniform spatial distribution.
  • All past information about the target is summarised by a single state estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}(k-1|k-1)}$ and its associated covariance $P(k-1|k-1)$
  • The observation pdf originating from the target is Gaussian with mean $\hat{z}(k|k-1)$ and innovation covariance $S(k)$.

PDAF algorithm

Two versions exist

  • Non parametric
    • A diffuse prior is used as knowledge for the number of false measurements
    • This requires no knowledge about the spatial density of false measurements
  • Parametric
    • A Poisson pmf is used for the number of false measurements.
    • Requires the spatial density parameter $\lambda$ (expected number per unit volume) of the false measurements.

Prediction

Predict the state and the observation:

\[ \begin{align} \hat{\mathbf{x}}(x+1|k) &= F(k)\hat{\mathbf{x}}(k|k) \\ \hat{z}(k+1|k) &= H(k+1)\hat{\mathbf{x}}(x+1|k) \end{align} \]

Calculate the predicted state covariance

\[P(k+1|k) = F(k)P(k|k)F(k)^T + Q(k)\]

The innovation covariance is

\[S(k+1) = H(k+1)P(k+1|k)H(k+1)^T + R(k+1)\]

The above steps are the same as in the standard KF/EKF.

Observations validation

A validation region(gate) is defined by the ellipsoid:

\[ \mathcal{V}(k+1, \gamma) = \{z: [z-\hat{z}(k+1|k)]^T S(k+1)^{-1} [z-\hat{z}(k+1|k)] \leq \gamma \} \]

where $\gamma$ is the gate threshold and can be calculated by the chosen gate probability $P_G$ and the chi-squared distribution lookup matrix.

This leads to a validated observations set

\[ \{z_i(k+1), i = 1, ..., m(k+1)\} \]

The innovation $\nu_i$ corresponding to the i-th validated observation is

\[\begin{align} \nu_i(k+1) = z_i(k+1) - \hat{z}(k+1|k) && i= 1, ...,m(k+1) \end{align} \]

The validation region volume $V$ is

\[V(k+1) = c_{n_{z}}|\gamma S(k+1)|^\frac{1}{2}\]

where $c_{n_z}$ is the volume of the $n_z$ dimensional unit hypersphere

\[ c_i = \left\{\begin{array}{ll} 2 & i = 1 \\ \pi & i=2 \\ \frac{4\pi}{3} & i = 3 \end{array} \right. \]

Probabilistic Data Association ~ non parametric version

The probability that the i-th measurement is the correct one can be calculated

\[\beta_i(k+1)= \left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{e_i}{b+\sum_{j=1}^{m(k+1)}e_j} & i = 1,...,m(k+1) \\ \frac{b}{b+\sum_{j=1}^{m(k+1)}e_j} & i=0 \end{array} \right. \]

where $\beta_0$ corresponds to the probability that none of the measurements is correct

The likelihood $e_i$ is

\[ e_i \overset{\Delta}{=} e^{-\frac{1}{2} \nu_i(k+1)^T S(k+1)^-1 \nu_i(k+1)} \]

\[ b \overset{\Delta}{=} \left(\frac{2\pi}{\gamma}\right)^{\frac{n_z}{2}}m(k+1)c_{n_z}^-1\frac{1-P_D P_G}{P_D} \]

Update

The state update can be carried by first calculating the weighted combined innovation

\[ \nu(k+1) \overset{\Delta}{=} \sum_{i=1}^{m(k+1)}\beta_i(k+1)\nu_i(k+1) \]

Then the state update follows

\[ \hat{x}(k+1|k+1) = \hat{x}(k+1|k) + K(k+1)\nu(k+1) \]

where the Kalman gain $K(k+1)$ follows the standard EKF/KF calculation.

The updated covariance is

\[ P(k+1|k+1) = P(k+1|k) - [ (1-\beta_0(k+1)) ] K(k+1)S(k+1)W(k+1)^T + \tilde{P}(k+1) \]

where $\tilde{P}(k+1)$ is responsible for spreading the updated estimate variance(increase uncertainty)

\[ \tilde{P}(k+1) \overset{\Delta}{=} K(k+1)\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m(k+1)}\beta_i(k+1)\nu_i(k+1)\nu_i(k+1)^T - \nu_i(k+1)\nu_i(k+1)^T \right]K(k+1)^T \]

Remarks on PDAF

  • $\tilde{P}(k+1)$ is positive definite and increases the covariance of the updated state, and is capturing the effect of origin uncertainty.

  • The estimation accuracy is data dependend.

  • In the parametric PDA , $\beta$ is calculated by replacing $m(k+1)$ with $\lambda V(k+1)$ , where $\lambda$ is the spatial density of the false measurements of the clutter model.

    The distinctive difference between the parametric and the non-parametric variants, is that the parametric asssumes an apriori known diffusion density $\lambda$ , where as the non-parametric one is calculating it based on the current number of validated observations $m(k+1)$ and the validation volume $V$.

  • While the structure is the same as in a linear filter, PDAF is a non-linear filter due to the weighted innovation calculation.

item.title: Thesis report Week#6
item.description:
Multisensor tracking - summary

When measurements are obtained from multiple sensors, there is a need to distinguish between different cases:

  • Asynchronous measurements - i.e the sampling times are different for each sensor and not necessarily fixed.

    In this project’s context, both the camera and the radar sensors are asynchronous, even though they can process the data at a fixed time interval, an actual target measurement might arrive at arbitrary time samples due to missed detections. The camera can become blinded, and the radar data processing algorithm can miss in extracting an observation.

  • Synchronized measurements - the sampling times of all sensors are the same (unrealistic)

Synchronized

In the case of syncrhonized measurements from different sensors, and the measurement noises among them are uncorrelated, which is a fair assumption, then the update process can be:

  • sequential - with the data from one sensor at a time
  • parallel - simultaneously by stacking the measurements vector

The approaches are equivalent for linear systems, but the second approach is computationaly more expensive because of the stacked covariance matrix inversion.

It is important to note that so far it has been assumed that there is no origin uncertainty for the measurements.

Measurements validation

The association problem

When tracking multiple objects, one needs to associate sensor observations, which at the moment of capture are origin agnostic , to a tracked target, before actually performing the filtering step.

The first step towards identifying an observations’s origin gating , that is to filter out unrealistic observations which most likely constitute outliers. This can be done in a probabilistic way by defining a validation region, or else a probabilistic hypervolume around the tracked target estimates.

A measurement within the validation region does not necessarily originate from the target the gate pertains to, but it is a valid association candidate . As soon as multiple observations belong to the same gate, then there is an association uncertainty .

Validation region

If one considers a tracked target, i.e the filter has already been initialized, then one should be able to calculate the predicted observation mean $\hat{z}(k+1|k)$ and its associated covariance $S(k+1)$ through the sensor’s measurement model and the filter’s estimate of the state.

A fair assumption at this point, is that the true measurement $$ z(k+1| Z^k)$$ conditioned on the past, is normaly distributed with its probability distribution function given by the standard Gaussian

$$ p[z(k+1)|Z^k] = \mathcal{N}[z(k+1); \hat{z}(k+1|k), S(k+1)] $$

Then the true measurement belongs to the validation region with probability

$$ \mathcal{V}(k+1, \gamma) = {z: [z-\hat{z}(k+1|k)]^T S(k+1)^{-1} [z-\hat{z}(k+1|k)] \leq \gamma } $$

The equation above describes a validation region or else a probabilistic hypervolume . In the simple case in which the measurement belongs to the three dimensional euclidean space $R^3$ then $\mathcal{V}$ defines an ellipse of probability concentration , that is the region of minimum volume that contains a given probability mass. The length of the ellipsoid semiaxes correspond to the square roots of the eigenvalues(singular values) of $\gamma S$.

The quadratic form in $\mathcal{V}$ implies that the validation region is chi-square distributed, with number of degree of freedom equal to the dimensionality $n_z$ of the observations.

In order to have a gating probability $P_g$, one must use the cumulative distribution function $F_{n_{z}}(\gamma)=P(X \leq \gamma)$ of $\mathcal{V}$ and since as already mentioned $\mathcal{V}$ is a chi-squared distributed function, the calculation of $\gamma$ is the solution to the following equation.

$$ P(X \leq \gamma) = P_g $$

Unfortunately no closed-form solution exists for the above equation since the function is highly non-linear, nevertheless look-up tables are available after point-wise evaluation.

One can verify that for the radar case, $df = n_z =3 $ degrees of freedom and if the probability that the true observation will fall in the gate is

$$P_{g} = P\{ z(k+1)\in\mathcal{V}(k+1,\gamma)\}=0.99$$

That means that an outlier’s probability $P_{\text{outlier}}$ will be

$$P_{\text{outlier}} = 1 - P_g = 0.01$$

then a possible solution for the gating threshold is

$$\gamma \approx 9.21$$

The square root $g = \sqrt{\gamma}$ is also referred to as “number of sigmas” or standard deviations of the gate.

The validation region thus, consists of points whose statistical distance to the predicted measurement is below a threshold $\gamma$. This is equivalent to the statistical innovation $[z-\hat{z}(k+1|k)]^T S(k+1)^{-1} [z-\hat{z}(k+1|k)]$ being below this threshold.

Measurements outside the validation region can be ignored, as they are too far and very unlikely(at least with probability $1-P_g$) to have originated from the target of interest. This argument holds, as long as the predicted observation is accurate enough.

item.title: Autonomous navigation: 31388
item.description:
Project outline

Software architecture

When thinking about a mobile robot, there are three key questions that need to be addressed.

  • Where am I?
  • Where do I want to go?
  • How do I get there?

In order to answer to these questions, the systems has to be able to:

  • Model the environment
  • Perceive the environment
  • Localise in the environment
  • Plan a trajectory and execute the movement

The simple robot demonstrated in the video is addressing the previous topics by

  • Perceiving the environment using a laser range sensor and a camera
  • Model the environment as straight line features on a map.
  • Localise using exteroceptive(laser) & proprioceptive(wheel encoders) measurements and Extended Kalman Filtering.
  • Plan a path using wavefront-planner.
  • Execute the path using a piecewise stable motion controller.

Environment perception & modelling

The world is described by hand-crafter features, that we as humans perceive as lines. The robot is extracting line features from the raw laser-point data by running a RANSAC algorithm.

Localisation

The robot is constantly using its kinematic model (differential drive) along with wheel encoder measurements to predict its traveled trajectory based on odometry, it is widely known that odometry world estimates are drifting due to a lot of effects which are either on purpose not being considered on the motion model, or they are not known. Extended Kalman filtering can reduce the estimate error of the robot’s pose in the environment with respect to a a-priory known map of feature points.

Obstacle avoidance

For path planning a wavefront-planning algorithm is being used:

Motion controller

The path planner is creating wheel velocity commands, based on a relative target pose. The controller is designed in polar coordinates as this provides a suitable framework to design an asympotically stable feedback controller.

item.title: Thesis report Week#4-5
item.description:
Updates:

This week I have been working on

  • Work on filtering visualization
  • Implement multiple moving objects in the Simulation
  • Implement multiple tracked objects in the simulation
  • Implement Particle Filter Algorithm for single target
  • Implement EKF with Probabilistic data Assosiation (PDAF)
  • Implement PF with Probabilistic Data Assosiation (PDAF)

[Multitarget-multisensor Tracking: Principles and Techniques : Yakkov Bar-Shalom Xiao-Rong Li]

Filtering visualization

A simple sailboat model is moving according to a deterministic Constant Turn rate and Velocity (CTRV) model. Given the known starting state of the target $x_0$ ,

  1. Initialize EKF at $x_0$
  2. Initialize PF at $x_0$
  3. Perform target deterministic motion update
  4. Obtain noisy observations (if any) $z$ from different rate sensors.
  5. Update the estimate of each filter with the given observations if any, using PDA.
  6. Plot the weighted particles above the PF state estimate where the height of a particle depends on its likelihood.
  7. Plot x-y uncertainty ellipsoids of the EKF around the state estimation.

Multiple moving objects

So far, the data association problem was not considered, that is the filtering algorithms were certain about the origin on the measurements. Data association is a major component of a tracking algorithm both because of the existence of multiple measurement sources, but also because of the existence of outliers in the measurements.

The approach implemented so far is a Probablistlic Data Association(PDA) algorithm. The goal again is in the update state, to associate measurements with different tracked targets based on a certain criterion described in the algorithm.

Mathematical explanation will be documented at a later stage, for now a visualization of the simulation is shown below.

  • Three different targets visualised
  • Both EKF & PF Filtering for each target
  • Triple crossing of targets, but the filters are able to associate correctly the measurements and correctly estimate the trajectories.

3d view

  • Continuous lines correspond to PF x-y state estimates for each target.
  • Magenta ellipsoids correspond to EKF state uncertainty
  • Point-clouds above the sailboats correspond to each target's particle distibutions where the height and size of a particle depends on its likelihood.(overall number of particles N=300 for each target).
  • Filters are associating the measurements to the correct targets at all times.

2d view

  • Continuous lines correspond to moving objects deterministic trajectories.
  • Blue scatter 'x' points correspond to the history of noisy radar measurements sensed from all the available targets.
  • Dashed lines correspond to the Particle Filtered x-y state estimate evolution over time. PDA is correctly associating the measurements to the measurement origin.

Particle filtering

Particle filters (PFs) belong to the category of sub-optimal filters. They represent probability densities with point mass densities ("particles"), and perform Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) estimations. SMC ideas were historically introduced in statistics from the 1950s but the limited computational power available at the time constrained their use and development. As soon as faster computers were made available, the related research activity in the field increased, resulting in their improvement and adoption in numerous applications, namely tracking, localisation of agents, pattern recognition, fault-detection, and ADAS.

Monte carlo integration

The basis of SMC methods is Monte Carlo integration. If a multidimensional integral needs to be evaluated:

\[\mathbf{I} = \int \mathbf{g(x)} d\mathbf{x}\]

where

\[ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{R}^{n_{x}} \]

Then the Monte Carlo (MC) approach for numerical integration assumes a factorisation

\[\mathbf{g(x)} = \mathbf{f(x)} \cdot \pi(\mathbf{x}) \]

so as that $\pi(\mathbf{x})$ is interpreted as a probability density which is satisfying

\[ \begin{align} \pi(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0 \\ \int \pi \mathbf{x} d \mathbf{x} = 1 \end{align} \]

If one can "draw" $N >> 1$ samples ${x^i; i = 1,...,N}$ distributed according to $\pi (\mathbf{x})$ , then the integral estimate can be approximated with the sample mean:

\[ I = \int \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})\pi(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = I_N \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^i) \]

Given that the samples $\mathbf{x}^i$ are independent, then $I_N$ is an unbiased estimator, and according to the law of large numbers it will almost surely converge to $I$.

Given that the variance of $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$, $\sigma^2$ is finite,

\[ \sigma^2 = \int (\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})- I)^2 \pi(\mathbf{x}) \]

then the central theorem holds and the estimation error converges to the distribution:

\[ \lim_{N\rightarrow \inf} \sqrt{N}(I_N - I) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \]

In the Bayesian context, $\pi(\mathbf{x})$ conveniently corresponds to the posterior belief density. What is not convenient though, is the fact that the posterior is usually multivariate, nonstandard and only known up to proportionality, thus distributed sampling is usually not available from it. A solution to this is the use of Importance Sampling.

Importance sampling

In order to generate samples directly from $\pi (\mathbf{x})$ and estimate $I$ using Monte Carlo integration, one can generate samples from a density $q(\mathbf{x})$, which is similar to $\pi(\mathbf{x})$. The pdf $q(\mathbf{x})$ is usually referred to as importance or proposal density.

Similarity between $\pi \text{ - } q $ is expressed as:

\[ \pi(\mathbf{x}) > 0 \rightarrow q(\mathbf{x})>0 , \text{for all } \mathbf{x} \in R^{n_x} \]

which means that $q(\mathbf{x})$ and $\pi(\mathbf{x})$ have the same support. The integral $I$ can be rewritten thus as:

\[ I = \int \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = \int f(\mathbf{x}) \frac{\pi(\mathbf{x})}{q(\mathbf{x})}q(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} \]

Applying the MC sample mean:

\[ I_N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^i) \tilde{w}(\mathbf{x}^i) \]

where the importance weights shape a probability mass function(PMF), and thus need to be normalised.

\[ w(\mathbf{x}^i) \frac{\tilde{w}(\mathbf{x}^i)}{\sum_{j=1}^N \tilde{w}(\mathbf{x}^j)} \]

Bayesian estimation

As already mentioned, in the Bayseian context, $\pi (\mathbf{x})$ is the posterior density. For selecting the proposal distribution $q(\mathbf{x})$ it makes sense to try to mantain a degree of similarity between the proposal and the posterior, so that the finite $N$ selected $\mathbf{x}^i$ samples fall into probable regions of the posterior, that is regions that contain information about the actual posterior distribution $p(\mathbf{x} | z_i)$.

Sequential importance Sampling (SIS)

[reference to 1.3 and conceptual filtering solution]

Intuitively, a recursive Bayesian nonlinear filter is a very suitable domain for applying MC integration. This sequential Monte Carlo approach is known with various names such as,

  • boostrap Filtering
  • condensation algorithm
  • particle Filtering
  • survival of the fittest

The key idea again, is to represent the posterior density function by a limited set of samples and their importance weights, based on which estimates can be calculated. As the number of samples increases, the sampled distribution becomes an equivalent representation to the functional description of the posterior pdf, and the SIS filter approaches the optimal Bayesian filtering estimator.

The recursive nature of SIS implies that the new weights describing the posterior $p(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{z}_k)$ are being updated based on their previous values, and the likelihood of a new measurement \(p(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k^i)\), i.e

\[ w_k^i \sim w_{k-1}^i \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k^i)p(\mathbf{x}_k^i | \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i)}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^i | \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i , \mathbf{z}_k)} \]

Then the posterior filtered density is described as a pmf as:

\[p(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{z}_k) \approx \sum_{i=1}^N w_k^i \delta(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^i)\]

The recursiveness of the algorithm is evident since the importance weights $w_k^i$ and support points $x_k^i$ are being propagated as each measurement is received sequentially. The SIS algorithm is the basis of most particle filtering algorithms, and the choice of the importance density function is crucial in the design.

Importance density selection

A popular and convenient selection of the importance density is the transitional prior, i.e. the motion model:

\[q(\mathbf{x_k | x_{k-1}^i , \mathbf{z}_k}) = p(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^i)\]

substituting the above in the SIS algorithm yields

\[ w_k^i \sim w_{k-1}^i p(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k^i) \]

Degeneracy problem

A general problem of a SIS particle filter is that the particle distribution becomes extremely peaked through the subsequent measurement updates. That is, the variance of the importance weights can only increase over time. This effect overtime leads to poor accuracy of the estimates and leads to a common problem of the SIS particle filter, that of degeneracy. An effective countermeasure is monitoring the level of degenracy of the filter and resample when appropriate. Such a measure is the effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ as introduced by [ref]

\[ \hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(w_k^i)^2} \]

It verify that \(1 < N_{\text{eff}} < N\) , thus a usual degeneracy criterion is

\[ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} &< \alpha N \\ \alpha \in \mathcal{R}: &\alpha \in (0,1) \end{aligned} \]

Resampling algorithms

Whenever a degeneracy is detected(i.e $N_{\text{eff}}$ falling below a threshold value), resampling is performed. Resampling follows the strategy of survival of the fittest, by eliminating particles in the set with low weight values and replacing them with copies of the more probable ones.

The resampling algorithm is thus:

  1. Normalize the particle set weights $w^j$
  2. Calculate the cumulative mass function \(F_k(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} w_k^j\).
  3. Draw $N$ random samples $u_i \in \mathcal{R}$, $i=1,..,N$ uniformly distributed on the interval $[0,1]$.
  4. For every uniform sample $u_i$, pick $\hat{x}_k^i = x_k^j$ out of the current samples pool, where the picking index $j$ depends on the weight's value as

\[ j = \underset{i}{\arg\min} |F_k(i)-u_i| \]

The above strategy leads to a weight selection survival chance equal to the weight's value $P(\hat{x}_k^i = x_k^j) = w_k^j$ . This results in useful samples having a higher likelihood of being selected for the new set. The new weights of the samples are uniform i.e. the weights are set to

\[\{\hat{x}_k^i , 1/N\}\]

The use of resampling technique might lead to other issues though. Diversity among particles is depleted as highly probable particles are through subsequent resampling steps genetically dominating the distribution. Moreover, if the process noise is too low then the distribution will not diversify enough in between measurement updates and will eventually become a uniformly weighted replicate of a few particles. This phenomenon is also known as sample impoverishment or particle depletion . [ref].

PF Algorithm outline

The PF algorithm thus summarises to:

  1. Initialise the filter by drawing particles according to an initial belief $p(\mathbf{x}_0)$

Usually \(p(\mathbf{x}_0)\) is Gaussian with known moments, or a uniform distribution around the initial estimate $\mathbf{x}_{0}$.

  1. Update the particle set weights according to a the likelihood of the new measurement.

This step requires pointwise evaluation of the likelihood-function $p(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k)$ which depends on the stochastic properties, of the measurement model.

Example: For the radar sensor, the likelihood function is the multivariate gaussian:

\[ p(z|x) = (2\pi)^{-\frac{k}{2}}\det{}(\mathbf{R})e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(z-h(x))^{T}\mathbf{R}(z-h(x)\right)} \]

Where $\mathbf{R}_{k\times k}$ is the measurement noise covariance matrix

  1. Normalise weights to represent a pmf.
  2. Resample if required, based on $N_{\text{eff}} < \textit{threshold}$
  3. Propagate every point according to the motion model.

It is crucial to use non-zero process noise at that step, as that ensures that additional diversity is being introduced into the distribution.

  1. Repeat from step #2

[ref]

item.title: Thesis report Week#3
item.description:
The essence of estimation

The generation of information that has the following properties

  • quality (accuracy/reliability) higher than the raw measurements
  • contains information not directly available in the measurements

[Multitarget-multisensor Tracking: Principles and Techniques : Yakkov Bar-Shalom Xiao-Rong Li]

  • No access to variables inside the first two blocks
  • The only accessible variable are the measurements which are affected by noise.

The estimator

Uses knowledge and processes the measurements to yield an estimate of a variable of interest(state or state uncertainty), while trying to make best use of the available data. In this process it is taking into account the:

  • System dynamics
  • Sensor Models
  • Prior information & probabilistic nature of the uncertainties

Tracking

Tracking is the processing of measurements or observations obtained from a target in order to maintain an estimate of its current state

The state of a target usually consists of:

  • Kinematics - position, velocity, acceleration , turn rate, etc. expressed w.r.t a coordinate frame
  • Feature components - target classificiation, spectral characteristics, radar cross-section etc.

Measurements or observations are noise-corrupted quantities which are related to the state of a target, such as

  • Direct estimate of position (GPS)
  • Range and azimuth(bearing) from the sensor (radar, cameras)
  • Range rate (radar)

The observations are not raw data sequences but usually the output of a complex signal processing subsystem.

Coordinate systems

Four coordinate systems are usually involved in tracking algorithms.

  • Geodetic
  • Local-tangential
  • Local spherical
  • Earth centered Earth-fixed (ECEF) coordinates

The transformations between the systems exist and are non-linear.

Geodetic to ECEF

GPS measurements usually arrive in geodetic coordinates, where as tracking is usually performed in the local tangential plane. The transformation between these two requires an intermediate transformation from geodetic $(\phi,\lambda,h)$ to ECEF $(X,Y,Z)$ coordinates.

\[ \begin{align*} x = 3 \\ y = 5 \end{align*} \]

\[\begin{align*} X &=(N+h)cos\lambda cos\phi \\ Y &= (N+h)cos\lambda sin\phi \\ Z &= [N(1-e^2)+h] sin\lambda \end{align*}\]

ECEF to local-tangential plane (ENU)

The local-tangential plane is the east-north-up rectangular coordinate system and it is determined by fitting a tangential plane to the surface of the earth at some reference point. The reference point is the origin of the frame. Such a system is convenient in the context of tracking nearby ships because the motion and measurement models reduce to two-dimensional problems.

Given a reference point in Geodetic $[\phi_0,\lambda_0]$ and the corresponding ECEF $[X_0,Y_0,Z_0]$ coordinates, then any other point's ECEF coordinates $[X,Y,Z]$ can be converted to local tangential plane coordinates $[x,y,z]$ using the following transformation

\[ \begin{bmatrix} x\\ y\\ z \end{bmatrix}= \mathcal{L}\left(\begin{bmatrix} X\\ Y\\ Z \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} X_0\\ Y_0\\ Z_0 \end{bmatrix}\right) \]

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a function of the reference point $[\phi_0,\lambda_0,h_0]$

\[ \mathcal{L} = \begin{bmatrix} -sin\phi_0 & cos\phi_0 & 0 \\ -sin\lambda_0cos\phi_0 & -sin\lambda_0sin\phi_0 & cos\lambda_0\\ cos\lambda_0 cos\phi_0 & cos\lambda_0 sin\phi_0 & sin\lambda_0 \end{bmatrix} \]

Body fixed frame (or local tangential own-ship frame)

This frame is the local-tangential frame rotated around the z-axis so that the x-axis is aligned with the own-ships heading direction from the true north $h_{self_{true}}$. This frame in case of a non rolling-pitching vessel coincides with the body-fixed frame. This is also called the sea-keeping frame because the vessel is assumed to move along the direction of its average velocity. Since this system is rotating along the vessel's heading direction and so do the local sensors, it is convenient to use for tracking purposes.

src: [Kinematic Models for Manoeuvring and Seakeeping of Marine Vessels]

The transformation thus is:

\[\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}_{ok}= R_{z}\]

\[\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}_{\text{w.r.t. own-ship}} = R_{z}(\frac{\pi}{2}-h_{self_{true}}) \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}_{\text{local tangential}} \]

where

\[ R_{z}(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix} cos\theta &-sin\theta &0\\ sin\theta &cos\theta &0 \\ 0 &0 &1 \end{bmatrix} \]

Purpose of using different coordinate systems

Both the motion and the measurement model equations depend on the coordinate system of choice. Sensor measurements from a radar usually arrive in the local spherical system, where as the GPS measurements either for the own-ship or for other vessels arrive in the geodetic system. It is convenient to express a target's motion model w.r.t the local tangential body fixed frame, since the local sensors like the radar and the cameras are rigidly attached to the body-frame. This simplifies the measurement models for local sensors, nevertheless it is creating the necessity to simplify the global measurement sensors like the AIS.

Motion model

When choosing a coordinate system for the target motion model, it is very convenient to assume the target's motion on the local-tangential plane with the origin placed on the own-ship(body fixed frame).

The motion models proposed in literature are numerous. Trying to keep the complexity low, linear motion models assume a constant velocity(CV) or constant acceleration (CA). These models have the advantage that the state transition equation is linear. Unfortunately they assume straight motions and are thus unable to model rotational motion. When one introduces the angular speed of the target around the z-axis, the resulting models are referred to as curvilinear models.

Use of the local tangential own-ship coordinate system

Tracking objects using the body-fixed planar frame is a fair assumption at sea since a target in the vicinity of the own-ship in this case can be projected on the self-body fixed frame, and is thus a fair approximation as long as the distance between the tracked target and the own-ship is not large enough for the earth's curvature to induce approximation errors. The model's fidelity using the body-fixed frame is a topic of debate. In the context of sensor fusion and local situational awareness, the on-board sensors used on a ship operate within a range limit that should --- at least intuitively --- ratify the tangential plane approximation.

When it comes to situational awareness in a more global context, i.e. tracking target vessels across the Atlantic, one could argue that there is no sense in applying sensor fusion since the targets are observable solely through GPS measurements.

The above is just an assumption at this point.

State vector

Assuming the target's motion on a plane, then two different state-space descriptions can be used depending on the expression of the velocity vector:

  • Cartesian velocity model \(\mathbf{x} = \left[x,y,\dot{x}, \dot{y},\omega\right]^{T} \)

  • Polar velocity model \(\mathbf{x} = \left[x,y,\upsilon, h,\omega\right]^{T} \)

where

\[ \begin{align*} \upsilon &= \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} \\ h &= arctan\left(\frac{\dot{y}}{\dot{x}}\right) \\ \omega &= \dot{h} \end{align*} \]

Where:

  • $(x,y)$ is the displacement relative to the local self-body fixed coordinate system

  • $\upsilon$ is the target's linear velocity

  • $h$ is the heading angle

  • $\omega$ is the turn-rate of the target

all expressed w.r.t the own-ship's body frame which has the x-axis aligned with the own-ship heading direction.

Continuous constant turn rate and velocity (CTRV) model

Starting with a time continuous model

\[\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = f(\mathbf{x}(t)) + w(t)\]

then assuming that the linear velocity \(\upsilon\) and the turn rate \(\omega\) remain constant --- which is a fair assumption for a moving vessel --- the non-linear dynamics are

\[f = \begin{bmatrix}\upsilon cos(h) \\ \upsilon sin(h) \\ 0 \\ \omega \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}^{T}\]

which is describing constant turn rate and velocity(CTRV)model.

Discretisation of the CTRV motion model

Since the system is simulated in discrete time, one can integrate the motion model within a sample time \(T\) and obtain exactly

\[ \mathbf{x}(t+T) = \mathbf{x}(t) + \int_{t}^{t+T}(f(\mathbf{x}(\tau)+w(\tau) )d\tau \]

and hence the discrete update model by ignoring the noise is

\[ \mathbf{x}(t+T) = g\left(\mathbf{x}(t)\right) \]

where

\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} \frac{2\upsilon}{\omega}sin(\omega T)cos(h + \frac{\omega T}{2})\\ -\frac{2\upsilon}{\omega}sin(\omega T)cos(h + \frac{\omega T}{2})\\ 0 \\ \omega T \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} , \omega \neq 0 \]

or

\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} \upsilon T cos(h)\\ \upsilon T sin(h)\\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} , \omega = 0 \]

Observation models

An observation model $z_k = h(\mathbf{x_k}) + w_k$ expresses the observations $z_k$ at time instances \(t_k\) given the state vector $\mathbf{x}$ . $w_k$ is assumed to be white gaussian noise with covariance matrix that depends on the specific sensor. The selection of the state vector $\mathbf{x}$ can lead to linear or nonlinear observation models.

Radar observation model

The radar observation model includes calculating the distance and bearing from the own-ship to the target in polar form $z_{radar} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho, \theta \end{bmatrix}^{T}$ , given the target's position $\begin{bmatrix}x_t, y_t\end{bmatrix}^{T}$ w.r.t the sensor's coordinate frame. If the radar's frame is aligned to the vessel's frame, then the radar measurement model can be expressed as:

\[ z_{radar}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \rho \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \\ arctan(\frac{x}{y}) \end{bmatrix} + w_{radar} \]

where the measurement noise \(w\) is assumed to be white, zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $R_{radar} = \text{diag}\left[\sigma^2_{\rho}, \sigma^2_{\theta}\right] $

A reasonable sizing of the noise variances can be

  • $\sigma^2_\rho = 10 \textit{m}$
  • $\sigma^2_\theta = 3 \frac{\pi}{180} [rad] $

GPS observation model

The GPS observation model calculates the target's relative position \(\begin{bmatrix}x, y\end{bmatrix}^{T} \) w.r.t the own-ship tangential coordinate frame given

  • The own-ship's geodetic coordinates $\begin{bmatrix}\phi_{0}, \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix}^{T}$
  • The own-ships sea-keeping heading angle from true north $h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}$
  • The target's measured geodetic coordinates $\begin{bmatrix}\phi, \lambda\end{bmatrix}^{T}$ which is usually available at sea through the Automatic Identification System.

\[ z_{GPS}(\mathbf{x},\begin{bmatrix}\phi_{0}, \lambda_{0}, h_{0_{\textit{TN}}} \end{bmatrix}^{T}) = \begin{bmatrix} x \\\\ y \end{bmatrix}_{GPS} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\\\ y \end{bmatrix} + w_{GPS} \]

The fact that the geodetic coordinates and the heading to true north of the self-vehicle is not included in the state-space representation leads to a linear measurement system, as the measurements are pre-transformed into the local-tangential plane w.r.t the self-vehicles heading direction and the noise is added on the the post-transformed measurements.

The AIS measurements can be transformed into local-tangential vessel coordinates by using the aforementioned transformations.

\[ \begin{bmatrix} x \\\\ y \end{bmatrix}_{\text{local tangential}} = R_z(\frac{\pi}{2}-h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}) \cdot F_1\left(F_2(\begin{bmatrix} \phi \\\\ \lambda \end{bmatrix}),\begin{bmatrix} \phi_0 \\\\ \lambda_0 \end{bmatrix}\right) \]

Where

  • \(F_1\left( \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}_{ECEF},\begin{bmatrix} \phi_0 \\ \lambda_0 \end{bmatrix}\right)\) is the transformation from ECEF to ENU, given the geodetic coordinates of the own-ship $\begin{bmatrix} \phi_0, \lambda_0 \end{bmatrix}^T$ as a reference point.

  • $F_2\left(\begin{bmatrix} \phi
    \lambda \end{bmatrix}\right)$ is the transformation from geodetic to ECEF coordinates

  • $R_z(\theta)$ is the transformation from ENU to sea-keeping frame.

AIS observation model

An alternative method to using the aforementioned GPS model is to leverage the fact that the tracked targets are in the viscinity of the own-vehicle, and thus small differences in geodetical coordinates can be linearly mapped to ENU differences and the other way around. x [src:"Wikipedia:Geographic coordinate conversion"]

By differentiating the model mentioned in the Coordinate systems section, one can confirm that for given own-ship geodetic coordinates $(\phi,\lambda)_{\textit{own-ship}}$

\[ \begin{bmatrix} dE\\dN\\dU \end{bmatrix}= \begin{bmatrix} (N(\phi)+h)\cos\phi &0 &0\\ 0 &M(\phi) + h &0\\ 0 &0 &1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} d\lambda\\d\phi\\dh \end{bmatrix} \]

Which can be solved for

\[ \begin{bmatrix} d\lambda\\ d\phi \end{bmatrix}= \mathbf{T}(\phi)^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} dE\\ dN \end{bmatrix} \]

Since the target's state is in the sea-keeping frame, one needs to transform it to ENU.

If $\mathbf{R}_z(h)$ is the Euler's angle transformation for a rotation $h$ around the z-axis, then the transformation from sea-keeping to ENU

\[\mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}} \]

given the own-ship's seakeeping heading from true-north $h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}$ is ,

\[\mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}} = \mathbf{R_{z}}(\frac{\pi}{2}-h_{0_{\textit{TN}}})\]

\[ \begin{bmatrix} dE\\ dN \end{bmatrix}= \mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}} \begin{bmatrix} X\\ Y \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{target wrt}_{{\textit{sea-keeping}}}} \]

and thus, by combining the above equations

\[ \begin{bmatrix} \phi\\ \lambda \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{target}}= \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{0}\\ \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{own-ship}} + \mathbf{T}(\phi_{0})^{-1}\begin{bmatrix} dE\\ dN \end{bmatrix} + w_{\textit{AIS}}=\]

\[ \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{0}\\ \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{own-ship}} + \mathbf{T}(\phi_{0})^{-1} \mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}(h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}) \begin{bmatrix} X_{\textit{target}}\\ Y_{\textit{target}} \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{wrt}_{{\textit{sea-keeping}}}}+w_{\textit{AIS}} \]

Where

  • \(\mathbf{T}(\phi)= \begin{bmatrix} N(\phi)\cos\phi &0\\ 0 &M(\phi) \end{bmatrix} \) is the transformation matrix from geodetic differences to ENU differences.

  • \(N(\phi) = \frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{1-e^2\sin^2\phi}}\) is the prime vertical radius of curvature

  • \(M(\phi) = \frac{\alpha(1-e^2)}{(1-e^2\sin^2\phi)^\frac{3}{2}}\) is the meridional radius of curvature

  • \(\alpha,\beta \) are the equitorial radius(6378.1370 km) and the Polar radius(6356.7523 km) respectively, as derived from the WGS-84 ellipsoid model.

  • \(e = \sqrt{1 -\frac{b^2}{a^2}}\) is the ellipsoid's eccentricity.

  • \(h = 0 \) since the own-ship is always floating on the geoid.

  • \(w_{\textit{AIS}}\) is white gaussian zero-mean noise with covariance matrix \(\mathbf{R}_{\textit{AIS}}\) depending on the noise intensity of the AIS source.

The above equation can be rewritten to the standard measurement model formulation

\[ z_{AIS}(\mathbf{x},\begin{bmatrix}\phi_{0}, \lambda_{0}, h_{0_{\textit{TN}}} \end{bmatrix}^{T}) = \begin{bmatrix} \phi\\ \lambda \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{target}} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{0}\\ \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} + w_{\textit{AIS}} \]

, which is a linear measurement model that is approximating the geoid with a spherical-linearization about the own-ship's geodetic location.

Camera model

Assuming a camera sensor and a pinhole model, with the camera z-axis aligned with the own-ship body frame

A top-view of the pinhole model seen in the figure below

Then the measurement model for the predicted pixel location of a target obtained from a deep neural-network classifier and localiser along the y-axis is found by the projecting a point in homogeneous coordinates

\[x_{\text{wrt}_{\text{sea keeping}}} =\begin{bmatrix}x, y, 0, 1\end{bmatrix}^T\]

to the finite camera frame given measured information $z_{gyro}$ about the rotational state of the own-ship w.r.t the sea-keeping frame:

\[ z_{\textit{cam}_{\textit{px}}}(\mathbf{x},z_{\textit{gyro}}) = \frac{\begin{bmatrix}-P_1-\\-P_2-\end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}}{ \begin{bmatrix} -P_3- \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}}+ w_{\text{camera}} \]

Where $\mathbf{P}$ is the projection matrix and $P_{i}$ it's rows

\[ \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix}-P_1- \\-P_2-\\-P_3- \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K}\mathbf{\mathbf{R}_{\textit{own-ship}}^{\textit{camera}}}[\mathbf{I} | \mathbf{-C}] \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{\mathbf{R}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}^{\textit{own-ship}}} &\mathbf{0}\\ \mathbf{0} &1 \end{bmatrix}\]

Because of the denominator this is a non-linear model, and a description of the matrices that comprise the projection matrix \(P\) is given below:

\(\mathbf{K}\)

is the intrinsic camera calibration matrix

\[\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}f m_x &0 &p_x \\ 0 &f m_y &p_y \\ 0 &0 &1 \end{bmatrix} \]

with

  • \(f\) is the intrinsic camera calibration parameter, focal length in mm
  • \(m_x, m_y\) are the camera's sensor pixel density in \(\frac{\text{number of pixels}}{mm}\) across the two different directions of the image plane.
  • \(p_x,p_y\) correspond to the position of the principal point of the image in pixel coordinates
  • \(w_{\textit{camera}}\) is white gaussian zero mean noise with variance related to the classifier accuracy, or any calibration errors in the extrinsic and intrinsic camera matrices.

$\mathbf{R}_{\textit{own-ship}}^{\textit{camera}}$

is the extrinsic camera rotation matrix that describes the orientation of the camera frame w.r.t. the own-ship.

$\mathbf{C}$

is the extrinsic origin of the camera frame w.r.t. the own-ship.

$\mathbf{R}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}^{\textit{own-ship}}$

Is the transformation matrix that corresponds to the roll-pitch rotation of the own-ship w.r.t the sea-keeping frame. Please note that the order of the rotations matters.

\[ \mathbf{R}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}^{\textit{own-ship}}=\left( R_{\textit{pitch}} R_{\textit{roll}}\right)^T \]

Where \(R_{\textit{pitch}},R_{\textit{roll}}\) are the standard rotation matrices for Euler's angles $z_{\textit{gyro}}=(\alpha,\beta)^T = (\textit{roll},\textit{pitch})^T$.

\[ R_{\textit{roll}}= R_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 &0 &0\\ 0 &\cos\alpha &-\sin \alpha\\ 0 &\sin \alpha &\cos \alpha \end{bmatrix} \]

and

\[ R_{\textit{pitch}}= R_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta &0 &\sin \beta\\ 0 &1 &0\\ -\sin \beta &0 &\cos\beta \end{bmatrix} \]

A basic assumption at that point, is that the own-ship is able to measure the angles $(\alpha,\beta)$ of the own-ship body frame w.r.t the sea-keeping frame. These angles are usually available on a ship by using one or multiple gyroscope sensors.

Kalman filter

Modeling Assumptions:

  • System's state evolution is linear and driven by
    • Known input
    • Additive process noise which is assumed to be a zero-mean white, uncorrelated process with known covariance \(Q(k)\)
  • Measurements - linear sensor function of the state with
    • Addditive measurement zero-mean white noise with known covariance $R(k)$
  • Initial state , a random variable with known mean(initial estimate) and covariance (initial uncertainty)

src

Under the above assumptions the Kalman Filter is the MMSE state estimator. If the random variables are not Gaussian, but one can still obtain their first two moments, then the KF is still the best linear MMSE estimator.

KF pitfalls in tracking

  • Usually the motion models are nonlinear
  • The measurement models are usually nonlinear
  • Unable to capture mode changes(target maneuveres)
  • Can't handle cross-correlated noise
  • Single target & unimodal

Extended Kalman Filter

The EKF is a suboptimal state estimation algorithm for nonlinear systems.

Modeling Assumptions:

  • Same as the KF except that the motion model and/or the measurement models are nonlinear functions of the state, note that the noises are still assumed to enter additively into the models.
  • First order Taylor expansion of the nonlinear functions are a reasonable approximation to the original functions

Taylor approximation of the CTRV motion models

A nonlinear motion model $x_{k} = f({x}{k-1})$ can be linearised around a known state $x{k-1}$

\[ f_{k-1} \approx F_{k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \nabla_{x_{k-1}} f_{k-1}^{T}(x_{k-1}) \end{bmatrix}_{x_{k-1} \rightarrow\text{latest estimate}}^{T} \]

and for the CTRV model this leads to

\[ F_{{CTRV}_{k-1}} = \begin{bmatrix}1 &0 &\frac{sin(h+T\omega) - \sinh}{\omega} & \upsilon \frac{cos(h+T\omega)-cosh}{\omega} & \upsilon\frac{sinh -sin(h+T\omega) + T\omega cos(h+T\omega)}{\omega^{2}}\\ 0 &1 &-\frac{cos(h+T\omega) - cos(\omega)}{\omega} &\upsilon \frac{sin(h+T\omega)-sinh}{\omega} & \upsilon\frac{cos(h + T\omega) -cosh + T\omega sin(h+T\omega)}{\omega^{2}}\\ 0 &0 &1 &0 &0 \\ 0 &0 &0 &0 &1\end{bmatrix} \]

similarly, a measurement model $z_k=h(\mathbf{xk})$ can be linearised around the latest motion propagated estimate $x{k}$ as

\[ h_{k} \approx H_{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \nabla_{x_{k}} h_{k}^{T}(x_{k}) \end{bmatrix}_{x_{k} \rightarrow\text{motion updated estimate}}^{T} \]

Thus the radar model that measures distance and bearing(given a CTRV state-space) is linearised as

\[ H = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} &\frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} &0 &0 &0 \\ -\frac{y}{x^2+y^2} &\frac{x}{x^2+y^2} &0 &0 &0 \end{bmatrix} \]

Sebastian Thrun, Wolfram Burgard, and Dieter Fox. 2005. Probabilistic Robotics (Intelligent Robotics and Autonomous Agents). The MIT Press.

Simulation results

  • Motion model CTRV, sample time T = 100ms
  • Measurement models, asynchronous measurements from:
    • Noisy Radar measurements: bearing and distance, Ts=5 s
    • Noisy Camera pixel measurements. Horizontal target pixel location on the image plane. Ts = 20s

Having a close look at a segment of the trajectory one can observe the effect of using a CTRV model.

  • Clearly the estimator is able to adapt to the step change in the turn rate but it is rather slow, as the model is supposed to be a constant turn rate one.
  • The red ellipses correspond to the state uncertainty as propagated through the motion model, without an available measurement update.
  • The green ellipses correspond to the uncertainty after incorporating a measurement update.

Filter performance

Performance graphs

The performance of this tracker is outlined in the following figures

From top to bottom:

  • Predicted target position $x_p$ and real target position \(x\) w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame
  • Predicted target position $y_p$ and real target position $y$ w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame
  • Predicted target heading velocity $\upsilon_p$ and real target heading velocity $\upsilon$ w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame
  • Predicted target heading velocity $h_p$ and real target heading velocity $h$ w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame
  • Predicted target turn rate $\omega_{p}$ and real target turn rate $\omega$
  • Absolute distance prediction error norm $|e| = \sqrt{(x-x_p)2+(y-y_p)2}$
  • Innovation norm $|v|$, is a measure of how accurate the filter is tracking the real target, an unbound innovation norm implies that the observations do not match the predicted trajectory anymore i.e the tracker diverged.
  • Innovation of the range measurements
  • Innovation of the range rate Measurements
  • Innovation of the bearing measurement
  • Innovation of the camera localiser measurements

Determinant of the covariance matrix P

Calculating the determinant $||P||$ of the predicted state covariance matrix provides an indication of the tracking uncertainty of the target or in other words a measure of the surface of the plotted uncertainty ellipses. As seen in the figure below, measurement updates from different sensors are able to keep the uncertainty low.

item.title: Semantic segmentation
item.description:
Driving scene image segmentation using Fully Convolutional Networks

Motivation

Self-driving cars are one of the hottest areas of research and business for the tech giants. What seemed like a science-fiction, a few years ago, now seems more like something which is soon to become a part and parcel of life. “soon to be” is because of the fact that even though companies like Tesla, Nissan, Cadillac do have self-driving car assistance software. They still require a human to keep an eye on the road and take control when needed. However, it is fascinating to see how far we have come in terms of innovation and how fast technology is advancing.

Other applications of semantic segmentation include and definetely not limited to:

  • Classification of terrain visible in satellite imagery
  • Medical imaging analysis
  • Industrial inspection

Background

Fully Convolutional Networks 1 provide a solution to the image segmentation task, which is trying to answer to the question of ’where’ is something instead of just ’what’. The FCN complements the CNN architecture by following up the ”downsampling path” with an ”upsampling path”. Upsampling methods can include both learnable and non-learnable parameters.

FCN extends the classic convolutional network pipeline such such that it can be used for full image predictions. The input to an FCN is an RGB image and its output is a pixelwise classification prediction. The initial layers of the pipeline are similar to a standard CNN architecture where the image is transformed into a latent representation that includes contextual information but most structural information is lost due to the nature of convolution.

  • The use of CNN layers in the vanilla FCN architecture is reducing the structural information in the image as data flows through the ”downsampling path”, hence upsampling the low-dimensional information in the ”upsampling path” creates coarse bounding areas around the objects.

  • The proposed architecture is leveraging the available structural information of the convolutional feature maps by forward passing information from their max-pooling layers to the up-sampling layers. Merging feature maps from various resolution levels helps combining context information with spatial information. 􏰀* The proposed architecture is leveraging transfer learning by initializing the convolutional layers from an accurate pre-trained classification architecture(VGGnet).

  • The convolutional layers can be frozen or trainable. Performance of the architecture has been evaluated for both cases.

  • The architecture enables end-to-end training of the network. We define different models by adding forward passing feature maps from various resolution levels.

    • FCN32s : The original FCN without max-pooling forward passes.
    • FCN16s : Forward passing max pooled information from conv4 to the output of upsample6.
    • FCN8s : Extends FCN16s by upsampling the FCN16s skip connection, then fusing it with the max-pooled output of conv3 and applying upsample8.
    • FCNs : Extends FCN8s by summing the pooled output of conv2 with the FCN8s skip connection(not illustrated in Figure 1). Figure 1 illustrates the FCN8s architecture, the FCN16s is derived by cutting the skip2, and FCN32s by cutting both skip1 and skip2.

Structural information is being extracted from the pooling layers of the down- sampling path. Then this information is being fused with the up-sampled information by element-wise addition before moving upstream. Batch normalization and dropout layers are being applied to the outputs of every convolution and up-sampling layer. ReLu was used as the activation function.

Dataset:

Camvid and Cityscape are free available labeled dataset.

  1. Cambridge-driving Labeled Video Database (CamVid) is a collection of video and images where each pixel is labelled with one of the 32 semantic labels. This dataset contains both fix-perspective videos (CCTV footage) and from a driving automobile. The footage from automobile makes this dataset a suitable choice for this project.
  1. Cityscape dataset contains labelled video and images from fix-perspective videos in the streets of different cities. The images are labelled with one of the 32 semantic labels. For the project, we would focus on the CamVid dataset recorded from automobile. Fix-perspective videos will not be investigated.

Training:

Different submodels of the original architecture were tested depending on the resolution of the information extracted from the convolutional layers. Namely FCN32s, FCN16s, FCN8s.

Loss and performance metrics

For the task of image segmentation pixel wise binary cross-entropy loss is used. It examines each pixels individually. Since the target vectors are one out of K encoded (total 32 class labels), the binary cross entropy is calculated using each encoded class and then averaged over all the 32 classes. This makes sure that each pixel is learning equally while training.

To compare the performance of the FCN, we will consider two measures : pixel accuracy and IoU (Intersection over Union)

$$\textrm{Pixel accuracy} = \frac{\textrm{No of pixels predicted accurately}}{\textrm{No of pixels}}$$

$$\textrm{IoU} = \frac{\textrm{Area of Overlap}}{\textrm{Area of Union}}$$

Model predictions

  1. arXiv:1411.4038v2 [cs.CV] 8 Mar 2015 Fully Convolutional Networks for Semantic Segmentation. ↩︎

item.title: OGM
item.description:
Occupancy grid mapping in MATLAB2018a

Test-case of occupancy grid mapping using real pose data, and laser measurements.

Getting Started

For a showcase you can copy the whole rep and run the script

occupancy_grid_mapping.m

The script will run by using the measurement file laser_0.log which includes laser sensor data as well as odometry data.

Description

Odometry pose data is treated as the real pose of the robot, in that sense there is no variance/uncertainty in the pose data, hence the robot belief distribution at every step is represented by a dirac centered at the robots pose. This assumption should make sense, since we are addressing exclusively a mapping problem which requires good knowledge or the robots position to create an accurate map. By relaxing this requirement, the problem gets transformed into a SLAM problem, which is beyond the scope of this example. Another assumption is that the environment is static, thus it can get only more certain(brighter), and not more uncertain(darker) that in the initialisation.

What is occupancy grid mapping

Occupancy grid mapping is a probabilistic representation of an environment. The environment is discretised into (here even) cells and the grid values represent obstacle uncertainty. In the example above, the darker the grid the more uncertain the representation. As the robot moves around getting laser range measurements, it “sheds light” into the map and brightens the free-space areas. This is done by ray-casting the measurements on the grid and decreasing the associated cell’s probability. This is a rough explanation omitting mathematical details. For a detailed and mathematically involved explanation please refer to a very nice book.

item.title: Moving object detector
item.description:
Motivation

Mobile robots need to be aware of moving targets in their environment, let alone they have to do it fast and using the least resources possible. The case of particular interests is the one of mobile robots operating in a factory environment, in which they have to constantly avoid other dangerous moving targets such as forklifts. The motivation for this algorithm was a company that wanted to implement a computationaly light solution since their main processing unit was already busy with running other computationally expensive tasks.

Constraints

Low computational efforts exclude grid-based method, because they are having a difficulty scaling to larger areas while keeping computations low and therefore, a continuous method had to be used. Inspired from the probabilistic estimation methods the algorithm can be summarized to the following steps.

Algorithm overview

  1. Mark polygon area as free space from t-1 scan measurement.
  2. Shrink polygon area according to sensor noise model in order to avoid false positives on step#3.
  3. Find scan points from the last scan t that are violating the free space from step#2
  4. Assign gaussian measurement model to each violation.
  5. Calculate overall probability distribution function.
  6. Filter out violations with a lower probability than a threshold.
  7. Calculate new PDF, given the confirmed violations.
  8. Cluster confirmed violating points to separate targets.
  9. Calculate expected value of every cluster as the moving targets location and assign it to a moving target object.
  10. Propagate possible previous moving target objects based on confirmed violations field.
  11. Repeat #1 for new scan sample.

Remarks

  • The advantage of this method is that is effectively capitalizing the low dimensionality of the laser data, to create a fast and robust estimator, in constrast to using camera based methods which are prone to classification errors/illumination noise.
  • It is worth noting that the “free-space” algorithm will not work for objects moving away from the laser frame, which is not necessarily a disadvantage, since generally objects moving away from the robot are not a safety concern.
  • Although the method is robust, it is generally noisy, due to the nature of violating points. By including a dynamical model of the moving targets, one could include an optimal estimator in the pipeline that smooths out the estimated position of the targets.

Next steps

item.title: Formula car aerodynamics
item.description:
Design and construction of FSAE car aerodynamic elements. Use of computational fluid dynamics and goal driven algorithms to optimize the geometries and setups. Initial geometry was designed in Solidworks , then a parametric CAD file was created in ANSYS. Spallart-Allmaras and K-epsilon turbulence models were used for the solver. The optimization algorithm altered variables such as absolute and relative elements pitch and relative and absolute element location and scaling, and tried to maximize the lift to drag ratio. The solver used was ANSYS-Fluent

Aristurtle, is a research student team of the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki. The team’s department is the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering of Aristotle University and its members are undergraduate students from various scientific fields. These include Electrical and Computer Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, Chemical Engineering, Architecture, Physics and Economics. Working under the pressure of demanding deadlines, the team’s members broaden their academic knowledge, theoretically and practically and gain great experience.

Aristurtle’s goal is to design and build electric race cars in order to compete in the international Formula Student competitions against universities from all over the world. Preparing towards this goal its members work in a fully structured professional environment. The team’s vision is to contribute to the upgrading of new technologies in the electric automotive field, in Greece and worldwide, by researching and getting in touch with high-level innovative products and pioneering ideas.

item.title: Plasma Actuators
item.description:
The capabilities of flow control using DBD were studied with a vision of being implemented in UAVs to alter flight characteristics such as power efficiency, lift off velocity, and provide abilities such as direction control without the use of moving surfaces.

HCUAV is a research project, whose aim was to design and build a Medium-Altitude-Long-Endurance Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (MALE UAV) in order to carry out civil operations in Greece. The consortium was made up of three Greek universities and three Greek industries, and the chief Engineer was prof. K. Yakinthos (AUTH). Starting from scratch, the HCUAV prototype (RX-1) made its first and successful flight, 36 months after the initiation of the project. The maiden flight took place at August 4, 2016, and lasted approximately 15 minutes. The attached video is an overview of the testing phase, which started right after the conclusion of the aerial vehicle construction and integration.
